1:
The Chronicler opens a double register, and the witnesses first map its architecture before they read a line of it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the whole plan: two long lists, the first of the valiant men who went over to David before Saul's death (vv.1-22), the second of the fighting men who anointed him king in Hebron (from v.23); and the first list falls into three companies — the Benjamite kinsmen of Saul who came to Ziklag (vv.1-7), the Gadites and men of Judah and Benjamin who joined him in the wilderness fastness, and the Manassites who fell to him on his return to Ziklag before Saul's last battle (vv.19-22). They note that Ziklag was first allotted to Simeon (Joshua 19:5), then fell to the Philistines and was assigned to David.

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fix the place and the moment: Achish king of Gath had given Ziklag to David as a safe retreat from the wrath of Saul, and it was there, while he "yet kept himself close," that these first defectors sought him out. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, presses the obscure Hebrew of that clause — David was "shut up," restrained within bounds from dread of Saul, or, as he allows, "banished from the presence of Saul" — and reminds the reader that these "helpers of the war" were the very allies who raided Geshur, Gezer, and Amalek (1 Samuel 27:8).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare notice into providence. While David was persecuted he had but six hundred men for his guards; yet even "while he kept himself close, because of Saul," not appearing to invite his well-wishers, God was inclining and preparing them to come over with seasonable succours. "Those that trust God to do his work for them in his own way and time shall find his providence outdoing all their forecast and contrivance." The war, Henry insists, was God's, and it was God who "found out helpers of the war," whose forwardness to act for the man heaven had designed for the government is recorded here to their honour. So the witnesses turn a dry superscription into a theology of hidden preparation: the throne is not yet David's, the king is still a fugitive barred from court, and already the LORD is quietly gathering the army that will make him king.
2:
The muster opens not with names but with weapons, and the witnesses dwell on the strange dexterity of these first recruits. The reformed John Gill notes simply that they were armed with bows, much used in the warfare of that age. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott render the Hebrew more fully: these were "drawers of the bow," ambidextrous men who could hurl stones and shoot arrows with the right hand and the left alike. Ellicott recalls that the left-handed slingers of Benjamin were famous from of old, citing the seven hundred chosen men who could sling a stone at a hair's breadth and not miss (Judges 20:16); the trained ambidexterity of Saul's tribe was proverbial, and David's first band inherited it.

A small point of kinship draws the two grammatical-historical voices together. The men are called "of Saul's brethren of Benjamin," and both Keil and Ellicott insist this means his fellow-tribesmen, not his near relatives; the phrase "of Benjamin" is added precisely to prevent the reader from supposing Saul's own family is meant, as the parallel usage later in the chapter confirms (v.29). These were men of the same tribe as the reigning house, and that is what makes their defection remarkable.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley alone asks why they came, and answers from the heart of the whole chapter. They were of Saul's own tribe, yet they were "moved hereto by God's spirit, by the conscience of their duty to David, and by their observation of God's departure from Saul, and of his special presence with David." Here Wesley names the spring that fed every company in the list: not ambition, not the fortunes of war, but a discernment that the LORD had left Saul and gone over to the son of Jesse. Matthew Henry, surveying the same men, had guessed at "a generous indignation at the base treatment which Saul, one of their tribe, gave him." Whatever the immediate motive, the witnesses agree that these skilled Benjamite archers, kinsmen of the king they abandoned, stand at the head of the roll as the first evidence that even Saul's own tribe could read the hand of God and act on it, crossing the deepest natural loyalty to side with the man God had chosen.
3:
A roll of names now follows, and the witnesses read it less for biography than for the geography and lineage the names betray. The reformed John Gill marks the chief of the band, Ahiezer, then Joash, sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite — a man of Gibeah in Benjamin, which was Saul's own birthplace, "sometimes called Gibeah of Benjamin, and of Saul," so that some of these warriors may have been the king's near neighbours and kindred. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the town: Gibeah of Saul, lying between Ramah and Anathoth (Isaiah 10:29), and he tracks the other names to their homes — Jeziel and Pelet the sons of Azmaveth, perhaps the warrior of Bahurim named among David's heroes (1 Chronicles 11:33), and Jehu "the Antothite," a man of Anathoth, the priestly city.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, add the deeper interest of the list — the men of Anathoth and, further on, the Korahites who descended from the Levite Korah, some of whom David would later set as guardians of the thresholds of the tent that housed the ark, because their fathers had kept the entrance of the camp of the LORD (1 Chronicles 9:18). The point the witnesses quietly register is that these Benjamite defectors were drawn from the very heartland of Saul's power — Gibeah, Gibeon, Anathoth, Azmaveth — the towns that should have been most loyal to the fallen house.

The sober catalogue does its own work, and the witnesses let it speak. The Chronicler is not merely counting swords; by naming the towns he shows how far the tide had turned, for the picked men of Saul's own countryside were slipping away to the outlaw at Ziklag. Gill's suggestion that some were the king's kindred, and Ellicott's careful placing of each village, converge on a single impression: the ground was giving way beneath the house of Kish while David was still a hunted man, and the record preserves the names of those who first dared to change sides. That the picked men of Gibeah and Anathoth and Gibeon — the very towns that had fed Saul's court — should stand at the head of David's roll is itself the witnesses' quiet commentary, for the erosion had begun in the heartland, and heaven's choice was drawing to itself even the near neighbours of the king it had set aside.
4:
Among the Benjamite band one man is singled out for rank, and the witnesses weigh what his title means. The reformed John Gill names Ishmaiah the Gibeonite, "a mighty man among the thirty, and over the thirty," a warrior of Gibeon — another town of Benjamin — who came with a company under his command. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the notice on several fronts. Gibeon belonged to Benjamin (1 Chronicles 9:35), and the previous verse's insistence that these were Benjamites proves that Ishmaiah was a Gibeonite only by domicile, not one of the old Gibeonite people in the strict sense.

The chief puzzle is his place among "the thirty." That corps, Ellicott observes, must be the famous band of David's heroes (1 Chronicles 11:25), yet Ishmaiah's name does not appear in that catalogue — perhaps because he died before the list was drawn up. And the phrase "over the thirty" he leaves deliberately open: it may mean that at one time he was captain of the band, or it may be merely comparative, marking him "a hero above the thirty" in prowess. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes the plainer sense, that Ishmaiah "came attended with thirty valiant Benjamites, and was their commander" — reading "the thirty" not as David's elite corps at all but as the retinue this one chief brought with him.

So a quiet difference runs beneath the verse: whether the thirty are the celebrated order of champions, into which Ishmaiah was reckoned though unlisted, or simply a company of thirty men he led over to David. Ellicott gathers one more name into the notice — Josabad the Gederathite, of Gederah in the lowland of Judah (Joshua 15:36), whom he tentatively identifies with the Zabad son of Ahlai counted among the thirty (1 Chronicles 11:41). The witnesses do not finally settle the rank, but they agree on the substance: a distinguished captain, valiant beyond the common measure, brought his own men to the fugitive at Ziklag, and the record honours him with a place at the head of the Benjamite roll even though the later catalogue of heroes would pass his name by.
5:
The list of Benjamites runs on through five more names, and though only two witnesses pause over them, the names themselves prove eloquent. The reformed John Gill notes the last of the group, "Shephatiah the Haruphite," as a native of Haruph or Hariph, and passes quickly on. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers, for these bare names carry the fingerprints of history and even of theology.

Two of them arrest him. "Jerimoth" is a genuine Benjamite name, borne elsewhere in the tribe's genealogy (1 Chronicles 7:7), which confirms that the company is rightly assigned to Benjamin. "Bealiah" is weightier still: it means "Baal is Jah," that is, the LORD is Baal. Ellicott takes it as evidence that in an earlier and innocent age the word Baal, "lord" or "master," could serve as a title of the God of Israel himself, long before it became inseparably fixed to the Canaanite idol; a devout Benjamite could bear such a name without reproach. The observation opens a small window onto how Israel's religious language shifted over the centuries, a title once neutral hardening later into a byword for apostasy.

The final name ties the muster to the returned exiles. "The Haruphite," Ellicott notes, points to the "children of Hariph" who are listed just before the "children of Gibeon" in Nehemiah's register of those who came back from Babylon (Nehemiah 7:24), the Hebrew margin here reading "Hariphite." So the clan that sent a warrior to David at Ziklag still had descendants centuries afterward among the men who rebuilt Jerusalem. The witnesses draw no dispute from these verses; instead they let the reader feel how a genealogist's dry column of names, when read closely, becomes a thread running from the wilderness of Judah down to the post-exilic community — the same Benjamite houses reappearing at the two great turning-points of the nation's life, the raising of David and the restoration after the captivity. Even the obscure members of David's first band, Gill and Ellicott together suggest, were not nameless make-weights but heads of families whose blood and memory the Chronicler was careful to preserve.
6:
Five more names close the Benjamite roll, and over the word "Korhites" the witnesses openly divide. The reformed John Gill is emphatic that these Korhites are "not Korahites, descendants of Levi," but the posterity of a Korah who belonged to Benjamin — a distinct family bearing the same name. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the opposite view without hesitation: these are, "without doubt," descendants of the Levite Korah, one branch of whom David afterwards made keepers of the thresholds of the tent that sheltered the ark, because their fathers had been watchers at the entrance of the camp of the LORD (1 Chronicles 9:18); the thoroughly Levitic names Elkanah and Azareel, they add, betray the family's priestly stock.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school as Keil, holds the two possibilities in tension. He first reads them as five members of the Levitical clan Korah — noting that "Elkanah" recurs in the lineage of Heman the Korhite musician and in the ancestry of Samuel (1 Chronicles 6:33), that "Azareel" is a priestly name (Nehemiah 11:13), and that there must have been Levites about the tabernacle at Gibeon. Yet he grants Gill's point in the same breath: "these Korhites may have been members of the Judean clan Korah" mentioned earlier (1 Chronicles 2:43), otherwise unknown. So the debate is threefold — Levites of Korah, Benjamites of a Korah, or a Judean Korah — and it turns on whether the name marks descent or merely coincidence.

Beneath the dispute the witnesses agree on the striking thing itself. Keil and Delitzsch suppose that the very service of these men as doorkeepers of the sanctuary "may have roused in them the desire" to seek out the LORD's anointed and cast in their lot with him. Whether Levite or Benjamite by blood, they close the first company with a note of consecration: men bound up with the guarding of holy things were among the first to guard the person of the coming king. The verse gathers the last of the ambidextrous archers — Elkanah, Jesiah, Azareel, Joezer, Jashobeam — and, with the two sons of Jeroham still to be named, brings the Benjamite muster to its close, leaving the reader to weigh whether these were priests turned soldiers or soldiers who merely shared a priestly name.
7:
The last two Benjamites are named with their father and their town, and the town raises a question the witnesses cannot leave alone. The reformed John Gill records "Joelah, and Zebadiah, the sons of Jeroham of Gedor," and at once feels the difficulty: Gedor was a city in the tribe of Judah. He offers two escapes — that it "might now belong to Benjamin," the borders having shifted, or that this was "another city of the same name" lying within Benjamin's own territory.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the tension out in full. On the one hand the names point to Benjamin: Jeroham is the name of a Benjamite clan (1 Chronicles 8:27), and two Benjamite chiefs are called Zebadiah (1 Chronicles 8:15). On the other hand Gedor was plainly a town of Judah, south-west of Bethlehem (1 Chronicles 4:4). How then does a Judean place-name stand at the foot of a list that purports to be all Benjamites?

Ellicott canvasses the answers and refuses the easiest. Some critics, he reports, "account for the appearance of Judaean names in a list purporting to relate to Benjaminites, by the assumption that the chronicler has welded two lists into one" — a splicing of separate documents that left the seam showing. But he prefers a simpler and more historical explanation: "towns did not always continue in the hands of the tribes to whom they were originally intended, and some Judaean towns may have contained a partially Benjaminite population." A border settlement could shelter families of both tribes, so that a man of Benjamin might well hail from a nominally Judean Gedor. The witnesses thus decline to convict the Chronicler of clumsy compilation. Where a modern eye sees a contradiction — Judah's town in Benjamin's roster — Gill and Ellicott see only the ordinary untidiness of tribal geography, populations mingling along the frontier long before David's day. The two sons of Jeroham close the Benjamite company, and with them the first of the three wilderness bands is complete, its very difficulties testifying, the witnesses suggest, to the antiquity and authenticity of the record rather than against it.
8:
The scene shifts across the Jordan, and a fiercer breed of warrior comes into view. The reformed John Gill explains the great phrase "separated themselves": these men of Gad, who lived on the far side of Jordan, cut themselves off from their tribe, their families, and the government of Saul to come over to David in his stronghold — whether in the wilderness of Ziph or Maon, or, as some think, at Ziklag. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes the separation pointed: they "separated themselves from the royalists of Gad, who clung to Saul," and sought David in one of his desert refuges — the cave of Adullam, the wooded hill of Hachilah, the crag of Maon, or the rocks of En-gedi (1 Samuel 22:1).

What captivates the witnesses is the poetry of the description. Ellicott reads the Hebrew almost as verse: "men of might, men of war fit for the battle," veterans wielding shield and spear, "and face of the lion, their face; and like gazelles on the mountains they speed." The style itself, he says, "betrays its ancient source"; the Chronicler is plainly borrowing from some contemporary record, and he compares David's own elegy over Saul and Jonathan, who "were swifter than eagles, they were stronger than lions" (2 Samuel 1:23), and the swiftness of Asahel, light of foot as a wild roe (2 Samuel 2:18). The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds that such swiftness "was considered to be a grand accomplishment in a warrior" throughout all antiquity.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the character behind the imagery. These Gadites, he says, unlike the distressed and discontented who had first gathered to David for mere protection (1 Samuel 22:2), were "brave men, men of war, and fit for the battle." They were swift "not to fly from, but to fly upon, the enemy," with faces like lions so that no man could out-fight them; and their leaving their own tribe he calls "a laudable separation," made though David was but in the hold in the wilderness. Henry finds in them a pattern of costly discipleship: "Those are fit to be employed in the cause of God that can venture" upon difficulty in dependence on divine protection. The witnesses converge on a portrait of eleven lion-faced men who abandoned kindred and country, crossed the river, and threw in their lot with a fugitive because they were sure of his cause.
9:
Eleven Gadite names are set down in order, and the witnesses read even the counting as significant. The reformed John Gill lists them — "Ezer the first, Obadiah, Eliab, Mishmannah, Jeremiah, Attai, Eliel, Johanan, Elzabad, Jeremiah, Machbanai; in all eleven" — and supposes they are ranked "according to their age, or merit, or order in coming to David." The ordinal attached to each name, "the first," "the second," "the third," gives the roll the cadence of a muster taken on the spot.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the leading title tersely: "the first" means "the chief," the same word rendered "head" of the band earlier in the chapter (v.3). Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, carry the numbering through to its climax in the subscription that follows, where these eleven are called "captains of the host," and they fasten on the astonishing clause that measures their prowess: "one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand." The words, they insist, do not mean that the least commanded a hundred men and the greatest a thousand, as the Vulgate wrongly rendered; the sense is that the weakest of these Gadites "could cope with a hundred," and the strongest "with a thousand" — a single warrior a match for a hundred foes, the echo of the covenant promise that five should chase a hundred and a hundred put ten thousand to flight (Leviticus 26:8).

The witnesses let that arithmetic do its theological work. Eleven men crossed the Jordan to a hunted outlaw, and the record reckons them not as eleven but as a host, each one worth a hundred or a thousand of the enemy. Keil and Delitzsch note that this reading is the one received by the older Jewish commentators and by the soundest modern judgment alike, against the mistaken Latin version; the point matters because it transforms the little band from a modest reinforcement into a decisive one. Gill's suggestion that the order reflects merit only deepens the impression — a graded company of champions, from Ezer the chief down to Machbanai the eleventh, every man of them a small army. The dry list of names, the witnesses show, conceals a claim about the LORD's economy: that he wins his victories not by multitudes but by a few men wholly given to his cause, so that David's growing strength is measured less by numbers than by the calibre of the men God was sending him.
14:
A subscription closes the Gadite list, and the witnesses treat its two clauses as a small crux of translation with a large point behind it. The reformed John Gill reads "captains of the host" as officers of the militia in their own country, men who commanded troops at home and became such afterward in David's army. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase literally, "heads of the host," that is, chief warriors, and turns at once to the harder line.

That line — "one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand" — the witnesses will not let be softened into a mere note of rank. Ellicott judges the margin correct against the text: the Hebrew says "one to a hundred, the little one; and the great one to a thousand," meaning that the feeblest of these Gadites was a match for a hundred men and the mightiest for a thousand. He grounds it in the covenant reckoning where one man of God puts a thousand to flight (Deuteronomy 32:30) and in the promise of Leviticus 26:8; and he remarks that the whole notice is "poetic, or at least rhetorical in character, and quite unlike the chronicler's usual style," another sign that an ancient song lies behind the prose. To sharpen the wonder Ellicott recalls that "David's band at this time was about 600 strong" — so eleven men, each worth a hundred or a thousand, were no small accession to so slender a force.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the practical weight of the title. These Gadites, he observes, "were officers of the militia in their own tribe, so that though they did not bring soldiers with them they had them at command, hundreds, thousands." The eleven came alone, but they came as commanders whose troops would follow; their defection carried, in prospect, whole companies of Gad behind it. The witnesses thus read the subscription as more than a formula. It fixes the calibre of the men — each a host in himself — and it hints at the reach of their coming, for captains bring armies. Gill's plain "captains," Ellicott's covenant arithmetic, and Henry's note on their command all point the same way: the LORD was strengthening his anointed not merely with brave individuals but with leaders through whom a tribe might turn.
15:
The Gadites' most daring feat is now told, and the witnesses gather about the flooded river with something like awe. The reformed John Gill sets the season: "these are they that went over Jordan in the first month," when the men had to cross the river to reach David on its western side. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the month as Nisan, answering to part of March and April, and reads the danger into it: the crossing came "before the snows on the mountains were melted, just as Jordan began to overflow its banks," or, if the banks were already breached, the greater the hazard and "the additional proof of their heroism."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes the swollen flood the whole point. The Gadites "did not wait till summer had made the Jordan shallow, but crossed it in spring, when perilously swollen with the rains and the melted snows of Lebanon" — the very time when Jordan overflows all its banks, as Israel had once found at the entry to the land (Joshua 3:15). And he explains the sequel, "they put to flight all them of the valleys," as meaning they made the hostile inhabitants of the lowlands flee before them, "both to the sunrise and the sunset," on both sides of the river. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the reading, carrying the same rout eastward and westward.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in the exploit a lesson in venturesome faith. These were "daring men, that could break through the greatest difficulties," who "swam over the Jordan, when it overflowed all its banks"; and, he adds, "those are fit to be employed in the cause of God that can venture thus in a dependence upon the divine protection." Having crossed, they did not do their work by halves: they put the enemy to flight "with their lion-like faces, and pursued them with matchless fury, both towards the east and towards the west." The witnesses do not divide; they weave one portrait of reckless devotion. Ordinary prudence would have waited for the ford to fall; these men breasted a river in spate to reach a fugitive king, and scattered whoever stood in their way. The flood that would have stopped lesser men only measured, for Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, and Henry alike, the depth of their resolve to be on David's side.
16:
A third company arrives, and the witnesses note the mingling of two tribes that had every reason to stay apart. The reformed John Gill records simply that "there came of the men of Benjamin, and of Judah" — men besides those already named — to David in the wilderness stronghold. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott points back to his earlier note on "the hold" (v.8), locating this arrival in the same desert fastness where the Gadites had found him.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, supply what the bare verse omits. This band of Benjamin and Judah came to David in the mountain-fastness, and their names are not preserved in the lists, "possibly because they were not handed down in the historical works made use of by the chronicler." At their head, as the next verse will show, stood Amasai, whom Keil reckons "chief of the thirty," the corps of David's heroes — though his name too is missing from the catalogue of champions. The older commentators, they note, conjectured that this Amasai was none other than Amasa, the son of Abigail, whom Absalom would later make captain of his rebel host in Joab's place.

What the witnesses feel in this short notice is the significance of the pairing. Benjamin was Saul's tribe, the last to be reconciled to David; Judah was David's own, yet had more than once betrayed him — the men of Ziph and of Keilah were Judahites who had sought to hand him over. That warriors of both these tribes should now come together to the hold is therefore no small thing, and it prepares the tension of what follows: David, so often deceived by his own kinsmen, cannot be sure whether these men come as friends or as a trap. Gill's plain record, Ellicott's cross-reference, and Keil's identification of Amasai all set the stage for the meeting at the mouth of the cave, where the fugitive will test the hearts of the very tribes that had most cause to love him and most history of failing him. The company comes; its welcome is not yet certain; and the reader is held, with David, between hope and suspicion.
17:
David does not fling the gate open. The witnesses read this verse as a study in wary discernment, a hunted man weighing whether the approaching band means help or betrayal. The reformed John Gill sees the caution plainly: David "went out to meet them," partly out of respect for men of eminence, but chiefly "out of suspicion, fearing they were not his friends," being largely of Benjamin, Saul's tribe, "and therefore was desirous of sounding them before he admitted them." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in David's speech and Amasai's reply "all the marks of a genuine survival of antiquity," and lays out the terms: "if for peace ye have come unto me to help me," then "I shall have toward you a heart for union," a heart at one with them; but "if ye be come to betray me to mine enemies," though "there be no violence in my palms," then "the God of our fathers behold and punish."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry expounds the fairness of the challenge. David, he says, "might well be timorous whose life was so much struck at; he might well be suspicious who had been deceived in so many," betrayed by the men of Ziph and Keilah though they too "were all men of Judah." Yet he deals openly: if they be faithful, "my heart shall be knit unto you; I will love you and honour you"; if false, he leaves them to God, "to be their avenger." Henry marvels that David, "a wise man," will not be judge in his own cause, and "a man of valour," will not avenge himself, but commits the matter to "the righteous God, who hath said, Vengeance is mine." The appeal is modest, not an imprecation but a submission: "The Lord look thereon, and judge, and rebuke it"; for, Henry adds, "the wrath of man works not the righteousness of God."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the title David chooses, "the God of our fathers — both his fathers and theirs." By it, Wesley says, David "minds them not to deal ill with him, for they were both descendants from the same patriarchs, and servants of the same God," and by it he encourages himself, trusting that God, being "a God to all Israel," would right him if he were abused. The witnesses converge: David tests the newcomers not with a threat but with an oath sworn on the common covenant, laying his innocence and his cause together in the hand of the Judge of all, and leaving the hidden intentions of Benjamin and Judah to be searched by the God who sees the heart.
18:
Then the Spirit falls, and the wary meeting breaks into confession. The witnesses treat Amasai's oath as the theological summit of the chapter, and they agree first on its source. The reformed John Gill says the Spirit "came upon" — or, as the Hebrew has it, "clothed" — Amasai, "a spirit of fortitude, of strength, courage, and presence of mind," with which he was covered as with a garment. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it "spirit clothed Amasai," and judges that the word "God" has dropped from the Hebrew, as the parallels show (2 Chronicles 24:20; Judges 6:34), where the same clothing-metaphor is used of the Spirit of the LORD. We may say, Ellicott allows, that Amasai spoke "under a sudden impulse of enthusiasm"; but "if we look deeper," he insists, "the impulses of spirit are spiritual, and enthusiasm for truth and right is indeed a sort of divine possession."

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the same point. Adam Clarke passes on the Targum's expansion — "the spirit of fortitude clothed Amasai" — and its rendering of the oath: "For thy sake, O David, are we come, that we may be with thee, thou son of Jesse; prosperity be to thee by night and by day, and prosperity to thy helpers, for the Word of the Lord is thy assistant." John Wesley guards against reducing it to natural courage: "not only saving graces, but other heroical and generous motions are ascribed to God's spirit," which here "stirred up in him a more than ordinary greatness of mind and resolution."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry unfolds the oath itself, clause by clause. Amasai professes cordial adherence — "Thine are we, David, and on thy side, thou son of Jesse" — and Henry notes the honour hidden in that last phrase: where Saul had called him "son of Jesse" in disdain (1 Samuel 22:7), these men use it to recall his descent from Nahshon and Salmon, princes of Judah. He wishes prosperity — "peace, peace be unto thee, and peace be to thine helpers" — "not drinking a health, but praying for peace." And he grounds it all in God: "for thy God helpeth thee," so that "those that have him for their God no doubt have him for their helper in every time of need." Henry turns the whole to devotion: from Amasai "we may learn how to testify our affection and allegiance to the Lord Jesus — his we must be without reservation, on his side we must be forward to appear and act." Gill adds the human tie that the Spirit seized upon, that Amasai was "a sister's son of David's." The witnesses close where the verse does: David, his suspicion answered by an inspired vow, "received them, and made them captains of the band."
19:
The narrative leaves the strongholds for the Philistine camp, and the witnesses must first untangle a delicate history. The reformed John Gill notes only that "there fell some of Manasseh to David," taking his part and joining him; but the grammatical-historical voices set the scene in full. Keil and Delitzsch explain that these Manassites went over "before the last battle of the Philistines against Saul," when David, sheltering with Achish, had been compelled to march out with the Philistine host against Israel — "but they helped them not," for the lords of the Philistines, upon deliberation, sent him back, saying, "He will fall to his master Saul to the jeopardy of our heads." The technical phrase "fall to," they observe, is the regular term for deserters in war who abandon their lord for the enemy (2 Kings 25:11), and the whole episode is the historical commentary given at length in the record of David's dismissal (1 Samuel 29:3).

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads the verse as a compressed summary of that Samuel narrative (1 Samuel 29:2 onward). He clears the ambiguity of "they helped them not" — it was David and his men who did not help the Philistines — and glosses "to the jeopardy of our heads" as "at the price of our heads": by betraying his new masters in the battle, the Philistine lords feared, David "will make his peace with his old master" Saul. Their suspicion, ironically, spared David the horror of fighting against his own people.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the hand of God in the whole arrangement. "Providence gave them a fair opportunity" to join David when he and his men marched back through their country; for these were "great men of Manasseh, who had no heart to join with Saul against the Philistines," and who "struck in with David, and very seasonably." Henry marvels at the timing: they were "not many, but they were all mighty men," and they came just when David would need them against the Amalekite raiders of Ziklag. The witnesses agree that the verse turns on a double deliverance — David is kept by the Philistines' distrust from lifting his hand against Israel, and is met on his homeward road by seven Manassite chiefs whose defection the LORD had timed to the very hour of his need. Gill's bare notice, Keil's and Ellicott's careful history, and Henry's providence combine to show a fugitive being spared one sin and supplied one strength in a single stroke.
20:
On the road back to Ziklag the Manassite chiefs come over, and the witnesses attend to the where and the when. The reformed John Gill distinguishes this company from any earlier one: "as he went to Ziklag, there fell to him of Manasseh" — men who joined David not when he marched out with the Philistines but "when he was dismissed from them, and was returning to Ziklag." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch name the seven — Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, Jozabad, Elihu, and Zilthai — and title them "captains of the thousands that were of Manasseh," heads of the tribe's military divisions.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, fixes the moment precisely: "on his dismissal by the Philistine princes, David returned with his men to Ziklag" (1 Samuel 30:1), and on the way "he was joined by the Manassite chieftains, probably before the battle which decided the fate of Saul and his sons." He notices the oddity that Jozabad is named twice, and judges "the repetition may be a scribe's error," comparing the name Jeremiah written twice over in the Gadite list (v.13). On the title "captains of the thousands" he adds a suggestive point of social history: the term "thousand" interchanges with "father-house" or clan, "and perhaps each clan originally furnished 1,000 warriors to the tribal host," so that these seven were the heads of great families, not merely officers over round numbers. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley confirms the setting in a phrase: it was "as he returned from the camp of the Philistines to Ziklag."

The witnesses let the geography carry the meaning. These were not fugitives seeking shelter but chieftains of Manasseh, leaders of thousands, who chose the very moment of David's rejection by the Philistines and Saul's approaching ruin to declare for the son of Jesse. Gill's care to mark them off as a second and later Manassite accession, Ellicott's dating of their coming to the eve of Gilboa, and Wesley's plain note of the homeward march all place the defection at the hinge of the two kingdoms — the old house falling on the mountains of Gilboa even as its captains' peers went over to the new. The Chronicler names the seven so that the reader may see how, at the darkest turn of David's fortunes, the LORD was sending him the strength of a whole tribe's leadership.
21:
These Manassites proved their worth at once, and over the manner of it the witnesses gently divide. The reformed John Gill takes the plain sense: "they helped David against the band of the rovers," that is, "the Amalekites, who, while he was gone with the Philistines, had seized on Ziklag, and burnt it, and carried the women captive"; coming to him just then, the seven chiefs "assisted him in his pursuit and defeat of them." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it identically — the help was "against the Amalekites who had taken and burnt Ziklag, whom David and his six hundred men were now pursuing."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, however, finds the Hebrew "brief and unintelligible" and offers a second construction. The common reading looks to the raid on Ziklag, where "the band" of Amalek burnt the town in David's absence (1 Samuel 30:8); but "why may we not render, 'And these helped David over the band,'" that is, "in the joint command of his forces"? On this view the phrase does not describe a battle against raiders at all, but the promotion of these captains to share command of David's little army, exactly as Amasai and his men were "made captains of the band" a few verses before (v.18). Ellicott presses the point because, he argues, the names enumerated through the whole passage "are those of captains and chiefs, not of ordinary warriors," so that these closing verses form "a subscription or concluding remark to the entire list."

So the witnesses leave two readings side by side: the Manassites helped David against the marauding band of Amalek, or they were set over his own band as commanders. The difference is real, turning on a single ambiguous preposition, yet it does not touch the substance both agree on — that these seven mighty men of valour, captains in the host, came at the hour of Ziklag's ruin and strengthened David when he most needed it. Gill and Wesley on the one hand and Ellicott on the other frame the same fact from two angles: whether in the field against Amalek or at the head of David's men, the Manassites made their defection immediately useful.
22:
The trickle becomes a flood, and the witnesses reach for the grandest image the chapter affords. The reformed John Gill dates the swelling of David's ranks to the days "after the defeat of Saul by the Philistines, unto the time that David came to Hebron," when help arrived "day by day." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew closely — "at the time of each day came people to David to help him, until it was a great host, like a host of God" — and explain the phrase as "a host which God has formed, and in which the power of God shows itself," comparing the "hills of God" and "cedars of God" by which Hebrew marks a thing as surpassingly great (Psalms 36:6).

Keil and Delitzsch guard the expression against the charge of exaggeration. There is here, they insist, no "idealizing historian" inflating the numbers, "for the greatness of a host of God is to be estimated according to the power and the spirit, not according to the number, of the warriors." David had at first four hundred, then six hundred, valiant men, "against whom Saul with his thousands could accomplish nothing." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, hears the same antique colouring, comparing Jacob at Mahanaim, who saw the angels of God and cried, "This is God's camp" (Genesis 32:2); the growing throng about David deserved the name because "ancient Hebrew denotes excellence by reference to the Divine standard." Ellicott adds that the verse "explains why David required so many captains as have been enumerated," and that it takes in "the considerable accessions which followed upon the defeat and death of Saul."

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses supply the simplest gloss and the widest wonder. Adam Clarke calls it "a very numerous army, like the army of the angel of God," and John Wesley, "innumerable, like the stars or angels, both which are called God's hosts." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the movement into a maxim of faith: "David's interest grew strangely just when he had occasion to make use of it; auxiliary forces flocked in daily, till he had a great host." And he draws the counsel: "When the promise comes to the birth, leave it to God to find strength to bring forth." The witnesses do not divide. They see in the daily-swelling camp the visible proof of the chapter's whole argument — that the LORD was making a king out of a fugitive, and that the army gathering to David was, in its spirit if not merely its numbers, nothing less than the host of God.
23:
The register turns from Ziklag to Hebron, from the fugitive's stronghold to the coronation, and the witnesses read the superscription as the pivot of the chapter. The reformed John Gill glosses "the numbers of the bands that were ready armed to the war" as men "trained up to it, and accounted for it." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott labour the ambiguous Hebrew word rendered "numbers of the heads": it may mean "polls," individuals counted by the head, or "sums," that is, companies and bands of soldiers. Both weigh against the Vulgate's and Septuagint's "chiefs of the army," Ellicott noting that "no chiefs are named in the list, except those of the Aaronites," so the heading cannot have turned the whole roster into a list of commanders. Ellicott adds that "to turn the kingdom" means to bring it round "out of the direct line of natural heredity," and that "according to the word of the LORD" points to what Jehovah had spoken by Samuel, virtually the word of his own mouth (1 Chronicles 11:3).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry surveys the whole assembly that the verse introduces. These were the men "active in perfecting the settlement of David upon the throne, after the death of Ishbosheth," who came to Hebron "to make David king," bringing "the quota which every tribe brought in ready armed to the war, in case there should be any opposition." Henry draws out the paradoxes the tallies contain: the tribes that "lived nearest brought the fewest" — Judah but six thousand eight hundred, Simeon seven thousand one hundred — "whereas Zebulun, that lay remote, brought fifty thousand, Asher forty thousand, and the two tribes and a half on the other side Jordan a hundred and twenty thousand." Yet he refuses to read coldness into the small numbers: the near tribes "showed as much of their prudence in bringing few, since all the rest lay so near within call, as the others did of their zeal in bringing so many," and the men of Judah "had enough to do to entertain those that came from afar." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the occasion in time — "after the death of Ish-bosheth, Saul's son" (2 Samuel 4:5). The witnesses thus open the second register with one accord: what follows is not a war-roll for battle but a coronation-roll, the armed representatives of all Israel converging on Hebron to fulfil, at last, the word the LORD had spoken over the shepherd of Bethlehem.
24:
Judah heads the tribal roll, and the witnesses find its modest number the first of the chapter's instructive surprises. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record "the children of Judah that bare shield and spear, six thousand and eight hundred, ready armed to the war," and gloss the phrase as "valiant men for the war service." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, notes that the whole list "proceeds from south to north, and then passes over to the trans-Jordanic tribes," beginning fitly with David's own tribe. And he answers the obvious puzzle — why so few from Judah? — with a point of history: "the tribe of Judah, which had acknowledged the sovereignty of David for the last seven years, had no need to appear in full force on the occasion of his recognition by the other tribes." Judah had crowned David at Hebron long before; its contingent here is token, not measure of its loyalty.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in a breath: the six thousand eight hundred "came hither in the name of their brethren, for that whole tribe stuck to David." They were a delegation, representing a tribe already wholly his. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had drawn the general rule that governs this figure and the next: the tribes nearest Hebron "brought the fewest," not from want of zeal but from prudence, since they "lay so near within call," and because the men of Judah, as hosts of the great gathering, "had enough to do to entertain those that came from afar."

The witnesses converge on a lesson the bare cipher would hide. A hasty reader might reckon Judah lukewarm, sending fewer men than half-tribes far to the north; the commentators show the reverse. Judah's smallness is the smallness of a tribe so secure in its allegiance that it needed only to send its captains, its heart having been given to David seven years before. Ellicott's historical note, Wesley's "in the name of their brethren," and Henry's principle of proximity all rescue the number from misreading. The muster begins, then, not with the greatest show of force but with the quiet confidence of the tribe from which the king had come — a delegation of six thousand eight hundred standing for a whole people whose fidelity was long since beyond question, and whose task now was less to fight for David than to welcome the rest of Israel to his crowning.
25:
Simeon follows its neighbour, and the witnesses read its figure by the same rule that governed Judah's. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record "of the children of Simeon, mighty men of valour for the war, seven thousand and one hundred," and Charles Ellicott, of the same school, refines the phrase: "mighty men of valour for the war" means rather men fit "for warfare, or military service" — seasoned soldiers, whatever their number. Seven thousand one hundred is a small contingent for a whole tribe, and the witnesses do not let it pass unexplained.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the reason, having laid it down at the head of the muster. Simeon was among the tribes that "lived nearest" to Hebron and therefore "brought the fewest," Judah but six thousand eight hundred and Simeon but seven thousand one hundred, "whereas Zebulun, that lay remote, brought fifty thousand." This was no coldness, Henry insists; the near tribes "showed as much of their prudence in bringing few, since all the rest lay so near within call, as the others did of their zeal in bringing so many." Simeon's towns were scattered within the territory of Judah itself, in the far south of Canaan; its men could be summoned at a moment, and so needed not to march in force to a coronation held on their own doorstep.

The witnesses thus turn a thin notice into a meditation on the different ways loyalty may show itself. A tribe far off proved its devotion by the length of its march and the size of its host; a tribe near at hand proved its devotion, no less, by sending a picked band of "mighty men of valour" while the rest stood ready within call. Keil and Delitzsch's plain record, Ellicott's insistence on "warfare" rather than mere numbers, and Henry's rule of proximity combine to guard Simeon, like Judah before it, from the charge of half-heartedness. The Chronicler is counting representatives, not measuring zeal by the head; and in the economy of the whole gathering, the seven thousand one hundred of Simeon are as truly a witness to the "perfect heart" of all Israel (v.38) as the fifty thousand of Zebulun. The small number, rightly read, testifies not to reluctance but to nearness and to trust.
26:
The tribe of Levi appears in arms, and the witnesses pause at the sight of priests and their kin marshalled for war. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the Hebrew carefully: "of the children of Levi" is literally "of the sons of the Levite," the definite article showing "that the name is gentilic or tribal here, not personal" — it is the whole tribe, not one man, that sends its four thousand six hundred. And he does not shrink from the strangeness of a martial Levi. "These martial Levites remind us of the priestly warriors of the crusades," he remarks; and he shows that it is no anomaly, for "that Levites might be soldiers, and in fact must have been such for the defence of the sanctuaries, is noted" elsewhere in the Chronicler's own pages (1 Chronicles 9:13) and in the arming of the Levites at Joash's coronation (2 Chronicles 23).

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, carry the number forward into the detail the next verses will unfold — four thousand six hundred Levites, among whom Jehoiada led the Aaronites and Zadok brought the chiefs of his father's house — treating this verse as the sum from which those companies are drawn. The witnesses agree that the figure gathers priest and Levite alike under the general name of the tribe, before the roll distinguishes the sacred families within it.

What the commentators draw from the notice is the striking willingness of the ministers of the sanctuary to stand with David in arms. The tribe set apart for the altar and the ark does not hold aloof from the making of the king; it sends its thousands, ready, if need be, to fight for the LORD's anointed as well as to pray for him. Ellicott's comparison to the crusading orders is apt but guarded — he does not commend holy war, only observes that a Levite bearing a shield was, in that age, no contradiction, since the guarding of holy things had always required armed men. The tribe whose portion was the LORD himself, and not a share of the land, here throws its weight behind the throne that would build the LORD a house; and the witnesses see in Levi's four thousand six hundred a sign that the coronation at Hebron was owned by the very custodians of Israel's worship, the crown and the sanctuary consenting together to the rule of the man after God's own heart.
27:
At the head of the Aaronites stands Jehoiada, and the witnesses take pains to say exactly who he was and was not. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch call him "the leader of the Aaronites," with three thousand seven hundred at his command, and identify his office precisely: he was "prince of the house of Aaron, head of the family of the Aaronites," but "not princeps sacerdotum," not the high priest, "which was a title appertaining to the high-priesthood, an office held at that time by Abiathar" (1 Samuel 23:9). They add the tradition, from Rashi and Kimchi, that this Jehoiada was the father of Benaiah, David's mighty man (1 Chronicles 11:22). Charles Ellicott, of the same school, confirms every point — "Aaron is used as the name of the leading clan of Levi," Jehoiada "was not high priest," but "head of the warriors of his clan" — and raises a careful question the text leaves open: "it is not clear whether the 3,700 are included in the 4,600 of the preceding verse or not. Probably not," so that the Levite total may be larger still.

The wesleyan-arminian voices echo the same distinction. Adam Clarke states flatly that "Abiathar was then high priest, and Jehoiada captain over the warriors of the house of Aaron," and John Wesley that Jehoiada was "not the high priest, for that was Abiathar, but one of eminent place under him." The unanimity is itself worth noting: every school insists that the man leading these three thousand seven hundred priests to Hebron held a military, not a pontifical, headship.

The witnesses guard the point because it matters for the picture of the whole gathering. The house of Aaron itself, the very family of the altar, sent a large armed company to make David king, under a prince of their clan who was a soldier and not the high priest — the high-priestly Abiathar being elsewhere occupied. The commentators leave a clear impression: the priesthood was not neutral in the choosing of the king. Its warriors marched to Hebron with the rest, so that the throne David was mounting had the swords of the sons of Aaron, as well as their prayers, arrayed on its side.
28:
Beside Jehoiada stands a young man whose name would outlast them all. The witnesses fix their attention on Zadok, "a young man mighty of valour," who brought with him "of his father's house twenty and two captains." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it "a youth, a valiant warrior," and identifies him with high probability as the priest who would rise to greatness — "perhaps the successor of Abiathar" in the high-priesthood, when Solomon at last thrust Abiathar from office (1 Kings 2:26). The twenty-two captains, "princes" of his father's house, Ellicott reads as proof of the strength of the priestly family at that moment: "the sub-clan or family of Eleazar must have been strong at this time to be able to furnish all these captains, and their implied companies of warriors." Yet, he observes, "the sum total of the Levites is not given" — the roll names leaders but leaves the full count of the tribe unrecorded.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, agree in the main and sharpen the genealogy. Zadok was "then still a youth," and may indeed be the same who was afterward "made high priest in place of Abiathar." His "father's-house" of twenty-two princes is "the Aaronite family descended from Eleazar, which was at that time so numerous that it could muster twenty-two family chiefs, who went with Zadok to Hebron." Where Jehoiada led the line of Ithamar's Aaronites, Zadok brought the rival and elder line of Eleazar, so that both great priestly houses were present at the crowning.

The witnesses leave the reader with a quiet foreshadowing. The obscure "youth" who appears here at the head of twenty-two captains is, on their reading, the founder of the enduring high-priestly line, the Zadok whose descendants would minister in Solomon's temple and whose name the prophet Ezekiel would honour long after. At David's coronation he is only a brave young priest bringing his kinsmen to Hebron; but the Chronicler, writing with the whole future in view, sets him beside the aged Jehoiada as if to mark the passing of the sacred leadership from one line to another. The strength of Eleazar's house, its twenty-two princes mustered for David, is the seed of the priesthood that would serve the throne through all the generations the king was about to establish.
29:
Benjamin's turn comes with an apology built into the count. The witnesses read its meagre three thousand as the most poignant number in the roll, and they explain it with one voice. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record that "of the children of Benjamin, the kindred of Saul," only three thousand came, "for hitherto the greatest part of them had kept the ward of the house of Saul" — that is, "were devoted to the interests of the fallen house." From this, they conclude, "we learn that the attachment of the Benjamites to Saul continued even after the death of his son Ishbosheth, and that it was with difficulty that they could bring themselves to recognise David as king." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads the notice the same way: "the Benjamites, as a whole, were still jealously guarding the interests of their own royal house," and the remark, like the phrase "Saul's fellow-tribesmen," "is intended to explain the comparative smallness of the contingent from Benjamin. The tribe's reluctance to recognise David survived the murder of Ish-bosheth."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley puts it in a phrase: they had "endeavoured to keep the crown in Saul's family." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the failing with a pastor's candour. Some of Saul's kindred did come over to David, he grants, "not so many as of the other tribes, because a foolish affection for their own tribe, and a jealousy for the honour of it, kept many of them long in the sinking interest of Saul's family." And he presses the warning home: "Kindred should never over-rule conscience. Call no man Father in this sense, but God only."

The witnesses converge on a tribe caught between blood and conscience. Benjamin had given Israel its first king, and could not easily bow to the second; loyalty to its own, once a virtue, had curdled into an obstinacy that held it back from the man the LORD had chosen. That three thousand came at all is, on this reading, a hard-won victory over tribal pride, the first breaking of a resistance that had outlived both Saul and his son. Keil, Ellicott, and Wesley explain the smallness; Henry moralizes it, turning Benjamin's grudging three thousand into a mirror for every reader tempted to let kinship or party override the claim of God. The little contingent stands in the roll as both a defection at last accomplished and a rebuke to the delay that made it so small.
30:
Ephraim musters twenty thousand eight hundred, and the witnesses ask why so great a tribe sent so measured a force. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record them as "mighty men of valour, famous throughout the house of their fathers," and gloss the last phrase as "men of name," renowned men (Genesis 6:4), reckoned "in their fathers'-houses." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, refines the rendering to "men of name, arranged according to their clans," and adds that "the phrase 'men of renown' is a natural addition to 'valiant heroes,'" their fame being "collective" — the honour of whole families, not merely of single champions.

The number itself catches Ellicott's eye. "The comparative smallness of Ephraim's contingent is noticeable," he writes; Ephraim was the leading tribe of the north, and one might expect it to head the northern muster with the largest force. He offers a historical conjecture to account for the shortfall: either the tribe "was already declining within the Mosaic period," as the wilderness censuses might suggest (Numbers 26:37), "or it may have been greatly reduced by the last wars of Saul with the Philistines," the disastrous campaigns that ended at Gilboa and left the northern tribes bleeding.

The witnesses draw from the notice a double impression. On the one hand, honour: these twenty thousand eight hundred were no common levy but "men of name," the renowned heads of Ephraim's clans, valiant and famous in their fathers' houses. On the other hand, a reminder of the cost of Saul's ruin: a tribe once foremost comes to Hebron diminished, perhaps thinned by the very Philistine wars that had brought the old king down. Keil and Delitzsch's insistence on their renown and Ellicott's note on their decline together give the verse its balance — Ephraim sends not a multitude but a company of distinguished men, its greatness now told in quality rather than in bulk. The Chronicler's tally, read with the commentators, quietly records both the enduring dignity of Ephraim and the wounds that Saul's fall had left upon the whole house of Israel, wounds that David's kingship was now to heal.
31:
The western half of Manasseh sends eighteen thousand, and the witnesses fix on the deliberate, public way they were chosen. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch specify "half Manasseh, this side Jordan" — distinct from the eastern half named later (v.37) — eighteen thousand "who were appointed by name, i.e., chosen as famous men to go thither and make David king." The phrase, they note, uses the same idiom as the numbering of the tribal princes under Moses (Numbers 1:17), and they draw a striking inference: "the tribe of Manasseh had consequently held a general consultation on the matter, and determined upon sending their representatives." This was no spontaneous rush but a considered, corporate decision, the half-tribe deliberating and then commissioning its chosen men.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, presses the same words to the same end. "Expressed by name," he explains, is literally "pricked down, or entered in a list, by names"; "the men had been levied by the tribal chiefs, and enrolled in lists for this particular service." The picture is administrative and solemn — a formal muster-roll drawn up, names inscribed, a delegation legally constituted to represent the tribe at the coronation.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley catches the courage hidden in the formality. To be "expressed by name," he says, meant these were men "who were not ashamed publicly to own David by putting their names to some paper presented to them for that purpose." The enrolment was a public act of allegiance, a signature against the fallen house, at a moment when Benjamin still clung to Saul and the outcome was not yet beyond dispute. The witnesses thus find in this middle number of the roll a lesson about the manner of loyalty. Where other tribes are simply counted, half-Manasseh is described as having chosen and named its representatives — a tribe taking counsel together, resolving for David, and sending men willing to have their names written down on his side. Keil and Delitzsch's "general consultation," Ellicott's enrolled lists, and Wesley's unashamed signatories all point to a deliberate, open, and courageous commitment, eighteen thousand men who came not by accident but by the settled and recorded decision of their people.
32:
Issachar sends the fewest men and the sharpest minds, and over the meaning of their famed "understanding of the times" the witnesses divide. The grammatical-historical voices take it as political sagacity. Charles Ellicott renders them "men sage in discernment for the times — critical junctures — so as to know what Israel ought to do," their chiefs two hundred, their clansmen "under their orders"; the old Jewish expositors, he grants, "concluded that the tribe of Issachar had skill in astrology, so that they could read in the heavens what seasons were auspicious for action, as the ancient Babylonians professed to do," but "all that the text really asserts is that those men of Issachar who went over to David thereby showed political sagacity." Keil and Delitzsch agree: the words mean men "experienced" in affairs, "knowing in insight," and refer "not to the whole tribe, but only to the two hundred heads"; they too report and reject the older notion of "astronomical and physical knowledge."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, by contrast, preserves the astronomical reading through the Targum, which he quotes at length: the sons of Issachar "had understanding to know the times, and were skilled in fixing the beginnings of years, the commencement of months, and the intercalation of months and years; skilful in the changes of the moon... astrologers in signs and stars, that they might show Israel what to do." John Wesley, of the same school, steers back to the political sense — they "understood public affairs, the temper of the nation, and the tendencies of the present events" — and shows their wisdom in action: they had "adhered to Saul while he lived," knowing the time was not yet come, and could not join David "while Abner lived," but "as soon as he was dead," they "owned David for their king."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers both threads without forcing a choice. The men of Issachar "had understanding of the times" — whether the "natural times," being weather-wise; or the "ceremonial times" of the solemn feasts; or, "rather, the political times," for "they understood public affairs." "It is the periphrasis of statesmen that they know the times" (Esther 1:13); "a stander-by sees sometimes more than a gamester." And Henry notes their weight in the tribe, "for all their brethren were at their commandment." So the debate stands — celestial science or shrewd statecraft — but the witnesses agree on the upshot: two hundred wise heads, followed by their whole tribe, discerned that the hour for David had come, and led Issachar to Hebron at exactly the right time.
33:
Zebulun's contingent is the model of the whole muster, and the witnesses dwell on the single-hearted loyalty its description praises. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the crowded Hebrew: fifty thousand "such as went forth to battle, expert in war, with all instruments of war," men who "could keep rank," and who were "not of double heart." The verb rendered "keep rank," they say, occurs only here and at the chapter's climax (v.38); against the Septuagint and Vulgate, which read "to help," its true sense is "to close up together, to set in order the battle array" — men who fell into formation "with not double heart." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees, translating "falling into rank without a double heart," and unfolds the idiom: the Hebrew "without a heart and a heart" asserts "the unwavering fidelity and resolute courage of these warriors," the opposite of the double-minded who "think one thing and say another" (Psalms 12:2).

Ellicott meets head-on the objection that fifty thousand is too many for so obscure a tribe. The numbers for Zebulun and Naphtali have been "thought surprising, because these tribes never played an important part in the history of Israel"; yet he answers with the Song of Deborah, where Zebulun "jeoparded their lives unto the death in the high places of the field" (Judges 5:18), and observes that the figures are "not discordant with those" of the wilderness censuses (Numbers 26:27).

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry press the moral of the "single heart." Wesley: they "were sincerely loyal, and did not dissemble with David, pretending to be for him, while in their hearts they favoured Saul's family; and none had any separate interests, but all were for the public good." Henry gathers Zebulun with the rest as "Israelites indeed, in whom was no guile." The commentators leave Zebulun as the pattern of what all Israel was becoming at Hebron — fifty thousand disciplined men, armed with every weapon, closing ranks in perfect order, and, better than their discipline, undivided in soul, their outward loyalty answering exactly to the loyalty within. In Zebulun, the witnesses agree, the single heart that the whole chapter has been reaching toward is first named outright, so that this remote and seldom-noticed tribe becomes the very emblem of the perfect heart with which all Israel would presently make David king.
34:
Naphtali brings its captains at the head of its thousands, and the witnesses note the peculiar shape of its muster. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record "of Naphtali a thousand captains, and with them, with shield and spear, thirty and seven thousand" — a great host, but reckoned first by its officers and then by its rank and file, as though the roll would honour the leadership before it counted the mass. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, attends to the weapon named: the word here for "spear" is a different term from the one used of Judah's men earlier (v.24), and "perhaps the former was thrown, the latter thrust" — a small philological touch that pictures the northern warriors armed with the casting-spear as well as the shield.

The number itself belongs to the pattern the witnesses have traced through the whole muster. Naphtali lay in the far north of Canaan, remote from Hebron; and, like Zebulun beside it, it sends a great force — a thousand captains and thirty-seven thousand men — where the near tribes of Judah and Simeon had sent only a few thousand. The distant tribes proved their zeal by the size of the host they marched so far to bring, exactly as Matthew Henry had explained at the head of the roll, that the remote tribes "brought so many" out of zeal while the near ones "brought few" out of prudence.

The witnesses let the tally speak for the reach of David's cause. From the northernmost border of the land, a full thousand of its commanders and thirty-seven thousand of its soldiers came down the length of Canaan to make a shepherd of Bethlehem their king. Keil and Delitzsch's careful separation of captains from men, and Ellicott's eye for the thrown spear, keep the notice from being a mere cipher; the roll is not counting an abstraction but a real army of the north, well-officered and fully armed, that had crossed hills and valleys for the coronation. In the great convergence upon Hebron, Naphtali's thousand captains stand for the truth the chapter keeps pressing — that the desire to see David king was not Judah's alone, nor the south's, but ran to the farthest tribes, who spent their strength and their distance to be present at the founding of his throne.
35:
Dan is not forgotten, and the witnesses make a point of saying so. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record "of the Danites, expert in war, twenty and eight thousand and six hundred," a substantial company of trained soldiers. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, notes the Hebrew form — "the Danite," singular and gentilic, "as in the case of the Levite" a few verses earlier — and adds the observation that gives the verse its quiet interest: "Dan is not omitted in the present list." The remark carries weight because Dan is elsewhere passed over in the Chronicler's genealogies, so that its presence here, armed and numbered among the tribes that crowned David, is worth marking against its frequent absence (1 Chronicles 7:12).

The witnesses read the inclusion as a sign of the completeness of Israel's consent. The Chronicler, so sparing of Dan elsewhere, here gives it a full and honourable place, twenty-eight thousand six hundred men "expert in war," ranged with the rest at Hebron. Keil and Delitzsch let the plain figure stand without comment, and Ellicott adds no more than his grammatical note; but the very restraint throws the weight upon the fact that the tribe is there at all. Whatever caused Dan's later eclipse in the registers, at this founding moment the tribe stood present and accounted for, its warriors as ready as any to marshal the battle for the LORD's anointed, and numbered fully among the companies that came to turn the kingdom to David.

The commentators thus turn a bare number into a testimony of breadth. The muster that makes David king is not a coalition of the favoured tribes only; it reaches even to Dan, the tribe most often missing from the sacred lists, and finds it willing and armed. Ellicott's insistence that "Dan is not omitted" is more than a textual note; it underscores that the whole house of Israel, without exception, came to Hebron. The kingdom about to be founded rests on the consent of all twelve tribes, and the Chronicler is careful that none, not even Dan, should be absent from the record of those who turned Saul's kingdom to David. Twenty-eight thousand six hundred trained men of a tribe elsewhere overlooked take their place in the roll, and by their very presence swell the witness that this coronation was the act of all Israel together, with a perfect heart.
36:
Asher closes the western tribes, and the witnesses read its forty thousand by the now-familiar logic of distance and zeal. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses "expert in war" with his recurring phrase, literally "to order or marshal battle," the same expression used of Zebulun and Dan (v.33); the margin's "rangers of battle," he says, "is good." These were men skilled to draw up the line and hold it, forty thousand of them. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, record the number plainly among the northern contingents — Asher, "such as went forth to battle, expert in war, forty thousand" — a great host from the coastal tribe of the far north-west.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had set the principle that explains the size. Asher, like Zebulun and Naphtali, "lay remote," and the remote tribes "brought so many" out of zeal, where the near tribes "brought few" out of prudence, since the near ones "lay so near within call." Asher's forty thousand, marching down from the borders of Phoenicia, are of a piece with Zebulun's fifty thousand and Naphtali's thirty-seven thousand — the far tribes measuring their devotion by the multitude they were willing to bring across the length of the land.

The witnesses find in Asher's contingent one more stone in the arch of Israel's consent. A tribe seldom prominent in the sacred history — its name rarely rising above the roll of genealogies — here sends a full forty thousand fighting men, expert to marshal the battle, to make David king. Ellicott's attention to the phrase "rangers of battle" keeps the verse from flattening into a cipher, and Henry's rule of distance gives the number its meaning. As the western tribes come to their close in Asher, the pattern the chapter has drawn stands complete on this side of the Jordan: near and far, prominent and obscure, southern Judah and northern Asher, every tribe present and armed. The commentators leave the reader on the threshold of the great trans-Jordan host still to be numbered, with the sense that the whole land, from Bethlehem to the Phoenician coast, had risen as one to set the son of Jesse on the throne.
37:
From beyond the Jordan comes the largest host of all, and the witnesses weigh both its size and the doubts it has raised. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the two-and-a-half eastern tribes together — "of the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and of the half tribe of Manasseh, with all manner of instruments of war for the battle, an hundred and twenty thousand." Charles Ellicott, of the same school, renders "on the other side" as "from the other side, that is, from Peraea," the trans-Jordan land, and equips them "with all kinds of weapons of warlike service." He does not hide the difficulty: "the large total of 120,000 for the two and a half Eastern tribes is certainly remarkable." Yet he declines to emend it, judging that "admitting the possibility of corruption in the ciphers here and elsewhere, the want of other documents, with which the text might be compared, renders further criticism superfluous." Where there is no parallel record to check the figure, honest caution leaves it standing.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley clears up a point of geography, noting that this half of Manasseh is the eastern half, "beyond Jordan," distinct from the western half already numbered (v.31). The two portions of Manasseh thus appear at opposite ends of the roll, one among the western tribes and one among the eastern, the whole tribe divided by the river yet united in coming to David.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already fastened on this vast number as the crown of his rule of distance: "the two tribes and a half on the other side Jordan" brought "a hundred and twenty thousand," the greatest force of all, because they lay the farthest off and could not be within easy call. Their zeal is measured by their multitude and their march. The witnesses let the huge total make its impression while refusing either to inflate or to explain it away. From the far side of the Jordan, from the pastoral lands of Reuben and Gad and eastern Manasseh, a hundred and twenty thousand armed men crossed the river to Hebron — the last and mightiest of the tribal contingents, sealing the testimony that all Israel, on both banks of the Jordan and to its remotest borders, had turned with one accord to make David king.
38:
The long roll gathers to a single confession, and the witnesses hear in this verse the whole chapter's heart laid bare. The reformed John Gill glosses "all these men of war that could keep rank" as "well-disciplined men," soldiers who held their order. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the summary in full: "all the above, being men of war, forming line of battle with whole heart, came to Hebron to make David king." The phrase "forming line of battle" repeats the verb used of Zebulun (v.33); but the weight falls on the words "with whole heart" — literally "with a whole heart," corde integro — which Ellicott would attach not to their marshalling but to their purpose: "they formed in line with fearless intrepidity," and came to Hebron of one undivided mind. And he presses the reach of the closing clause, "all the rest also of Israel were of one heart to make David king": "the rest," a word used here only, means those "who did not appear personally at Hebron," so that the sentence declares the whole nation, present and absent alike, "unanimous" (2 Chronicles 30:12). Ellicott reckons the total assembled "upwards of 300,000," and defends the figures against the sceptic, since "in those days every able-bodied man was, as a matter of course, trained in the use of arms," so that "man and warrior were almost convertible terms."

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, carry the same reading, joining "keep rank" and "perfect heart" as the two marks of these men who "came with a perfect heart to Hebron, to make David king over all Israel," while "all the rest also of Israel were of one heart." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this the verse he has been building toward. It is said of them all, he writes, "that they engaged in this enterprise with a perfect heart," and of Zebulun in particular "that they were not of double heart"; "they were, in this matter, Israelites indeed, in whom was no guile. And this was their perfection, that they were of one heart; none had any separate interests, but all for the public good."

The witnesses close the muster on that note of unanimity. The scattered companies of the whole book — Benjamite archers, lion-faced Gadites, the tribes near and far — are drawn together into a single description: men who could keep rank, who came with a perfect heart, and behind whom stood the one heart of all Israel. What began at Ziklag with a handful of defectors to a hunted man ends at Hebron with a whole nation of one mind; and Gill, Ellicott, Keil, and Henry agree that this perfect and undivided heart, more than the three hundred thousand swords, is the true glory of the day that made David king.
39:
The muster becomes a feast, and the witnesses let the coronation break into three days of joy. The reformed John Gill notes that "there they were with David three days, eating and drinking," "keeping a festival, to testify their joy on this occasion." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the "festive meals" the gathered tribes held, the eating and drinking that marked a great national celebration (compare the feasting at other turning-points of the history, 1 Kings 1:45), "for their brethren had prepared for them" — the nearer tribes providing the provision for the rest.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds a needed measure of realism and then rises to praise. "These were the deputies of the different people," he cautions; "it is not possible that all the thousands mentioned above could have feasted with David for three days," though a great number did, the nearer tribes of Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali having "brought all the necessaries for such a feast." From the whole scene Clarke draws his conviction that "the great majority of the tribes of Israel wished to see the kingdom confirmed in the hands of David; nor was there ever in any country a man more worthy of the public choice." He breaks into a tribute: "As a statesman, warrior, hero, poet, and divine, he stands unrivalled in the annals of the world; by him alone were the Israelites raised to a pitch of the highest splendour."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the deepest note in the "joy in Israel" the feast expressed. Those who had travelled least, he observes, "thought themselves obliged to be at so much the more charge in entertaining the rest, that there might be something of an equality"; a noble feast was made, "for there was joy in Israel, and good reason, for when the righteous bear rule the city rejoices." Henry turns the three days' banquet into a figure of the soul: "when the throne of Christ is set up in a soul, there is, or ought to be, great joy in that soul; and provision is made for the feasting of it, not as here for two or three days, but for the whole life, nay, for eternity." The commentators leave the reader with a united and joyful Israel at table with its new king — the gladness of a people that has, at last and with a perfect heart, received the man after God's own heart.
40:
The joy overflows the camp, and the witnesses close the chapter on the abundance that the tribes poured out at Hebron. The reformed John Gill notes that "they that were nigh," those who "bordered on the tribe of Judah," brought supplies. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch spread out the whole provision: not only the nearest tribes but even the distant men of "Issachar and Zebulun and Naphtali," on the far northern frontier, "brought bread on asses, and on camels, and on mules, and on oxen, and meat, meal, cakes of figs, and bunches of raisins, and wine, and oil, and oxen, and sheep abundantly, for there was joy in Israel." This joy, they explain, "moved those who remained at home to show their sympathy with the national festival solemnized at Hebron by sending the provisions" — the whole land contributing to the coronation feast, the masses of dried figs and raisin-cakes being the staple travelling-food of the country (1 Samuel 25:18).

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, adds the touches of an eyewitness's realism. The beasts of burden are "asses, camels, mules, oxen, but not horses," for these "were the usual beasts of burden in rocky Canaan"; the fare is "food of flour" and "cakes of raisins," "masses of dried figs and raisins" that "were, and are, a staple article of food in the East" (Amos 8:11). And he hears in the plain catalogue the ring of great antiquity: "the simple diction of the narrative, reminding us of Homer's feasts, is a mark of its ancient origin." The homely list of loaves and figs and wine-skins is, to Ellicott, the very signature of an old and authentic record.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the closing scene into its lasting meaning. The feast was made "for there was joy in Israel, and good reason, for when the righteous bear rule the city rejoices"; and he turns it to the reader's own heart, where "when the throne of Christ is set up in a soul, there is, or ought to be, great joy," with provision made "not as here for two or three days, but for the whole life, nay, for eternity." The witnesses end the chapter as it deserves to end — not with a battle but with a banquet, the tribes streaming toward Hebron laden with the produce of their fields, the whole of Israel keeping holiday because the man God had chosen was at last its king. The muster that opened with a handful of defectors slipping away to a fugitive closes with a nation feasting in the open, its long division healed, its joy running over in bread and wine and oil, poured out with a perfect heart before the throne of David.
